title: The 'If I Had More Money I Would' Trap
subtitle: A reading on the deferred life plan and why waiting for money first keeps both the money and the life on hold
excerpt: A reading on the deferred life plan, the belief that real life begins once money is sorted. Explains why deferring the things you want until you have more money tends to keep both the money and the life on hold, and what the shift looks like.

content.text: The "if I had more money I would" sentence is one of the most common money patterns there is. The holiday that waits for the raise. The business idea shelved until there's a runway. The thing you actually want, deferred to a future version of your finances that may or may not arrive. This reading frames the deferral mechanism and walks a journal-prompt practice for identifying what specifically is being held.
Why it works as a trap

The trap has two mechanisms. First, deferring what you want creates the internal experience of scarcity, the exact energetic state that keeps money away. Your baseline is one of not having; functionally, that's what persists. Second, the deferred thing is often evidence of the identity you're waiting to inhabit. The holiday, the course, the studio space are markers of the version of yourself that already has the money. Waiting for the money first reverses the causal order; identity usually comes before external reality matches it.
The difference between deferral and practicality

Not all waiting is the trap. Saving a deposit for a house is practical planning. The trap is when the waiting is indefinite, tied to a threshold that keeps moving, or attached to things that could be done now in a smaller form. If the sentence has been there for three years and the threshold hasn't been reached, that's the pattern.
The practice
Five journal prompts for naming what is being deferred and why. Five minutes per prompt against a timer.
1. What are three "if I had more money I would" sentences I am currently running?
Write the three. The specific ones. The trip, the course, the move, the rest. The actual deferred things.
2. How long has each of those been on the list?
A year, three years, ten? Write the years. Notice which ones have been there longest.
3. What's the smallest version of each that I could do right now?

Not the full version. The smallest practical version that would touch the same identity. A day trip instead of a fortnight away. A library book instead of a course. A weekend retreat instead of the studio.
4. What identity is the deferred thing a marker of?

What kind of person owns / does / takes that thing? Write the identity, not the activity. "Someone who travels for pleasure." "Someone who invests in their own growth." "Someone whose work has space."
5. What would actually change if I let myself live a version of that identity now, at a smaller scale?
Practical change and felt change. Not theoretical, the actual difference in the body of stopping the deferral, even at a smaller scale.
After the prompts
Pick one of the three sentences from prompt 1 and do its smallest version this week. The deferral pattern breaks through action at any scale, not through waiting for the right scale.
Where this practice comes from
Original to homemade.education. The piece draws on identity-first manifestation writing. It also draws on the receiving threads in MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program by Rebecca J Page.
